name: Little Bear River
short_description: Public BLM land along Little Bear River offering free rockhounding for quartz and chalcedony specimens. Gentle terrain and year-round access make this an accessible site for collectors of all levels.
description:
Little Bear River in Cache County offers rockhounders a prime opportunity to collect quartz and chalcedony specimens on public BLM land without fees or permits. The site sits at 5,093 feet elevation along a gentle slope that makes access comfortable for families and beginners. The Little Bear River drainage has exposed sedimentary and metamorphic formations that weather to reveal quality quartz specimens, chalcedony nodules, and occasional colorful jasper fragments in the stream gravels and cutbanks.

The geology of this region reflects Utah's complex Basin and Range formation combined with ancient Precambrian metamorphic basement rocks. Quaternary alluvial deposits along the river contain reworked mineral material from upstream sources, concentrating collectible specimens in accessible locations along the riverbed and adjacent washes. The gentle terrain and stream-cut banks provide natural exposure of productive layers.

Expect to find specimens ranging from small thumb-sized pieces to larger hand specimens suitable for lapidary work. The river gravel tends to concentrate harder materials, making systematic searching of bars and terraces productive. Water access and relatively flat walking make multi-hour collecting sessions feasible.

As a free public BLM site, Little Bear River attracts rockhounders seeking quality material without commercial pressure or entrance fees. The combination of productive geology, easy access, and no-permit requirements makes this a reliable collecting location throughout much of the year.
history: The Little Bear River drainage sits within the Bear River Range, part of the larger Wasatch-Uinta system shaped by Laramide orogeny and Basin and Range extension. Precambrian metamorphic rocks form the core, with Paleozoic sedimentary sequences uplifted and exposed along the valley. Quaternary alluvial processes have reworked and concentrated mineral-rich deposits in the river system, creating the accessible collecting zones found today.
tips: Search the active stream channel and elevated terraces where river gravels have concentrated heavier minerals like quartz and jasper. Focus on fresh cutbanks and exposed alluvial faces where weathering has loosened specimens. Bring a 5-gallon bucket and work upstream from the main parking area to follow productive material. Look for milky white quartz chunks and banded chalcedony nodules - specimens typically range from walnut to fist-sized, but larger pieces occur in deeper gravels.
terrain_notes: Gentle slope terrain at 5,093 feet elevation along the Little Bear River valley. Rolling hills with mixed sagebrush and grass cover. Walking surface consists of river gravel, sand, and turf - easy footing for most hikers. Stream access is moderate, with banks ranging from 3 to 8 feet high. No significant elevation gain required for productive collecting areas.
best_season: April - October. Spring through early fall offers the most reliable access and comfortable collecting conditions. Winter snowfall can make roads impassable and collecting difficult. Summer heat is moderate at this elevation. Late spring and early fall provide the best balance of accessibility and pleasant weather.
written_directions: From Logan, take US 89 north for approximately 8 miles. Turn east on State Route 16 (Logan Canyon Road) and continue for about 12 miles toward Greenville. Watch for Forest Service Road signs and BLM access markers. Turn north on the secondary road toward Little Bear River drainage (road names vary on local maps). The main collecting area is accessible via 2-3 miles of improved dirt road with several pullout options along the river. High-clearance vehicles recommended during wet conditions, but standard passenger cars can typically reach productive sections.
road_conditions: Improved dirt road with washboard surface during dry season. May become muddy during spring runoff or after rain. High-clearance or 4WD recommended during wet conditions. Typical passenger vehicles can access main collecting areas under normal conditions.
parking_notes: Multiple informal pullouts along the river corridor. No developed parking lot. Parking available in wide spots along the access road and informal clearings near the riverbank. Space for 3-8 vehicles at each of several locations.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per Federal Land Policy and Management Act (FLPMA). No commercial collection or resale. No motorized excavation. No sifting screens or dredging devices. Hand tools only (hammer, chisel, bucket). Collect from public BLM land only - respect private property boundaries.
rules:
Personal use collection only
Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA
No commercial collection or resale allowed
No motorized excavation equipment
Hand tools only (hammer, chisel, pick)
No sifting screens or dredges
Collect only on BLM land - respect private boundaries
Pack out all trash
nearest_services: Logan, Utah - approximately 20 miles south. Full services including gas, food, lodging, and equipment shops. Garden City (small community) approximately 15 miles northeast with limited services.
accessibility_notes: Relatively flat, gentle terrain accessible to most hikers and families. Informal dirt road to parking areas with uneven surface. Stream banks vary in steepness but generally manageable for moderate fitness levels. No wheelchair access to collecting areas. Sturdy footwear recommended for river gravel navigation. The site is suitable for older children and seniors with normal mobility.

faq.question: What gems and minerals can I find rockhounding at Little Bear River in Utah?
faq.answer: Little Bear River is productive for quartz specimens, chalcedony nodules, and jasper fragments. You'll find white and milky quartz chunks ranging from walnut to fist-sized pieces, plus occasional banded chalcedony and colorful jasper in the river gravels and stream cutbanks. These specimens are ideal for collectors at all skill levels.

faq.question: Do I need a permit to rockhound at Little Bear River, Utah?
faq.answer: No permit is required. Little Bear River sits on public BLM land where recreational rockhounding is allowed under FLPMA regulations. Personal use collection is free - you just need to follow the 25 lb per day limit and hand-tools-only rule.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to go rockhounding near Logan, Utah?
faq.answer: April through October offers the best collecting conditions. Spring through early fall provides reliable road access and comfortable weather at the 5,093 foot elevation. Winter snowfall can make roads impassable. Summer heat is moderate, and late spring and early fall offer the most pleasant conditions for extended collecting sessions.

faq.question: Is Little Bear River rockhounding site beginner and family friendly?
faq.answer: Yes, Little Bear River is excellent for beginners and families. The gentle terrain, easy vehicle access, and productive quartz and chalcedony specimens make it ideal for first-time rockhounders and children ages 6+. No permit or fee requirements lower the barrier to entry, and the site typically requires minimal hiking.

faq.question: What tools and equipment do I need for rockhounding at Little Bear River?
faq.answer: Bring a small hammer or crack hammer, flathead chisel, 5-gallon bucket, and safety glasses. Hand tools only are permitted - no powered equipment or dredging devices. A backpack, sturdy shoes for river gravel, and water bottle are essential. A field guide to minerals helps identify specimens in the field.

faq.question: How far is Little Bear River rockhounding site from Logan, Utah?
faq.answer: Little Bear River is approximately 20 miles north of Logan. From downtown Logan, take US 89 north for about 8 miles, then turn east on State Route 16 toward Greenville. Secondary roads provide access to the BLM collecting areas along the river corridor, roughly 12 miles from the route 16 turnoff.

meta_title: Little Bear River Rockhounding - Cache County, Utah
meta_description: Free BLM rockhounding for quartz, chalcedony, and jasper near Logan, Utah. No permit required. 25 lb daily limit. Family-friendly access.
